source.title: Brief an Richard Friedenthal vom 9. Mai 1941, SZ-AAP/B2.33

additional.context:
## Dieses Dokument

- Titel: Brief an Richard Friedenthal vom 9. Mai 1941, SZ-AAP/B2.33
- Signatur: SZ-AAP/B2.33
- Sprache: Englisch
- Objekttyp: Brief
- Umfang: 11 Scans

pages.text:
Hotel Wyndham 42 West 58 street
My dear friend, I just recieved your good letter and am
proud of your courage. You may imagine how anxiously
we are following all the events in London - finally they have
understood here the value of speed and I hope that the Ame-
rican help will be soon effective. I have written today to
Mrs Kaiser and asked her for a rendez vous, how she could
help you in your coming. The difficulty is that I have nobo-
dy for an affidarit. My publisher, my other friends have
given too many and their guarant[y] is no more accepted;
on the other hand Mrs Kaiser will perhaps find someone
who will forward it when he gets a personal guaranty from
a respectable person which I hope still to be. I am nearly sure
we can provide together something, please be sure that I shall
not falter in my efforts. Then comes still the question of the
passage but for that you will be active yourself. Here in
America you would with your splendid English find soon
your way; if ever you can write or try to write short-stories
(especially war stories of London would be effective) there is
the strange case that with a short story you can earn more
than with a whole novel.
As to my books. The Brazilbook will be published in August.
The Vespucci was first 200 pages and went in the detail
of the discussion. I found it a very dull book and interes-
ting only to geografers, so I cut it down to about 90 pages
which are amusing and coloured; perhaps we make a limited
pages.notes: Handschriftlicher Brief auf Hotelpapier. Tinte verblasst an einigen Stellen, aber gut lesbar.

pages.text:
edition with maps and portraits out of it. I hope to have
solved the problem in a quite sensible way - you know
that half of the geografers say that he was nothing but a liar
and the other half praise him as a genius. The solution is that
he did not swindle but that his books are not written by him
but compiled by editors without scruples who [?] used his
material and stuffed it with inventions to make it more in-
teresting (like with so many memoirs - Casanova for in-
stance - which were lengthened by ghost writers.) Perhaps I can
compare it with Bellinas letters from Goethe and Beethoven,
which were partly authentic partly fakes. But the whole story
is delightful how without his knowing he was declared the
discover of America and this country named after him.
Next week I make my first public appearance in
one of these horrid 1000 person-dinners. But we have to
raise money and so for our PEN club [eingefügt] Rauschning, Romains, Sigrid
Undset, Maeterlinck, Dorothy Thompson will speak - I have
ten minutes sharp and it is not easy because the three words
freedom, victory, democracy will have been used already three hundred
times by the others. I speak especially about our difficult posi-
tion to be apart the [?] regime and out of the German
nation for ever but within the language for ever - a few
remarks about our personal tragedy. But [eingefügt] all has here to have a
strong optimistic tendency and how to mix all this together
in ten minutes sharp (the radio wants precise time) makes
me some headache: how to give an accent of optimism to just
our personal case!
pages.notes: Handschriftlicher Brief, Fortsetzung. Text gut lesbar.

pages.text:
I am sending you a moral affidarit[?] saying
how long I have known you but I shall try to provide a
more[eingefügt] affidarit one still as I am not American. I hope to send
you this days a food parcel, they are allowed now (whereas-
ly in Brazil the consulate said to us that it created trouble
with the customs but from here all is so provided that the
parcels are directly forwarded.) You know that Hannah is only
happy to forward you all material help and please do not
wait to inform here whatever you wish. Dont wait till to a
real need. I wish that just in those times you had no mate-
rial worries whatever, that one of the very few things which
make me pleasure is that I am able to help my friends in
England (while here I cannot do it and you do not imagine
what cables come from Vienna, but how to pay fares round
the world.) We will go for the summer to some small place
and I hope to finish there the autobiography - a voyage to
Brazil[?] is in this critical times not advisable and by sea[eingefügt] have
it cost a fortune. I have seen here few people and we live
a very retired life, knowing every day, that I have to be
thankful to be able to do my work. Kindest regards to you
and come with a finished book! Yours ever
Stefan Zweig
pages.notes: Handschriftlicher Brief, Fortsetzung. Text gut lesbar.

pages.text:
Copy

To whom it may concern

I know and admire R I C H A R D F R I E D E N T H A L not only
as one of the best among the exiled German writers but also as
a man of excellent character and reliability. He has been my friend
since more than twenty years and there has never been a moment where
I was not proud of having his friendship. I know that Thomas Mann
and other writers do not estimate less than I do and we all would be
only too happy if a man of his extraordinary knowledge and eminent
capability and with such a blameless character could be with us here
in America.

signed: S t e f a n Z w e i g

New York, May 1941.

Richard Friedenthal Esq.
23 Ladbroke Gardens
London W11
England
pages.notes: Adressseite eines Luftpostumschlags. Poststempel 'NEW YORK MAY 9 1941'. Archiv-Aufkleber 'EXAMINER 4092'.

pages.text:
I am sending you a moral affidarit[?] saying
how long I have known you but I shall try to provide a
more[eingefügt] affidarit one still as I am not American. I hope to send

confidence_notes: Der Text ist sehr gut lesbar, die Handschrift ist klar. Einige wenige Wörter sind aufgrund der kursiven Schreibweise leicht unsicher, aber im Kontext eindeutig.
